sections.headline: The Parable of the Wedding Feast

verses.text: Jesus spoke to them again in parables, saying,
“The kingdom of heaven is like a king who prepared a wedding feast for his son.
He sent his servants to call those invited to the feast, but they refused to come.
Again, he sent other servants, saying, ‘Tell those invited: “Behold, I have prepared my feast; my oxen and fattened cattle are slaughtered; everything is ready. Come to the wedding feast.”’
But they disregarded the invitation; one went to his farm, another to his business.
The others seized his servants, abused them, and killed them.
When the king heard this, he was enraged. He sent his armies, destroyed those murderers, and burned up their city.
Then he said to his servants, ‘The wedding feast is ready, but those invited were not worthy.
So go into the highways and invite everyone you find to the wedding.’
The servants went out into the highways and gathered all they found, both good and bad, and the wedding hall was filled with guests.
But when the king came to see the guests, he noticed a man without a wedding garment.
He said to him, ‘Friend, how did you get in here without a wedding garment?’ The man was speechless.
Then the king said to the servants, ‘Bind him hand and foot and throw him into the outer darkness, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.’
For many are called, but only a few are chosen.”
Then the Pharisees went and plotted how to trap him in his words.

They sent their disciples to him, along with the Herodians, saying, “Teacher, we know you are truthful and teach the way of God with integrity. You are not swayed by others, for you do not show partiality.
So tell us then, what do you think? Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar or not?”
But Jesus, aware of their malice, said, “Why are you testing me, you hypocrites?
Show me the coin used for the tax.” So they brought him a denarius.
He asked them, “Whose image and inscription is this?”
They said, “Caesar's.” Then he said to them, “Give to Caesar what is Caesar's, and to God what is God's.”
When they heard this, they were amazed, and they left him and went away.
That same day the Sadducees, who say there is no resurrection, came to him and questioned him,
“Teacher,” they said, “Moses said that if a man dies without children, his brother must marry his wife and raise up offspring for his brother.
Now there were seven brothers among us. The first married and died without offspring, so he left his wife to his brother.
The same happened to the second, and then the third, down to the seventh.
Finally, the woman also died.
So in the resurrection, whose wife will she be of the seven? For they all had her as a wife.”
Jesus answered, “You are mistaken, not knowing the Scriptures or the power of God.
For in the resurrection, they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like the angels of God in heaven.
But concerning the resurrection of the dead, have you not read what God said to you,
‘I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob’? He is not the God of the dead, but of the living.”
When the crowds heard this, they were amazed at his teaching.
When the Pharisees heard Jesus had silenced the Sadducees, they gathered together.
One of them, an expert in the law, tested him with a question:
“Teacher, which is the greatest commandment in the law?”
Jesus replied, “‘Love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your mind.’
This is the first and greatest commandment.
And the second is like it: ‘Love your neighbor as yourself.’
All the Law and the Prophets are founded on these two commandments.”
While the Pharisees were gathered together, Jesus asked them,
“What do you think about the Christ? Whose son is he?”
They replied, “The son of David.”
He said to them, “How then does David, speaking in the Spirit, call him ‘Lord,’ saying,
‘The Lord said to my Lord, “Sit at my right hand, until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet”’?”
And no one could answer him, and from that day no one dared to question him anymore.

footnotes.content: The wedding garment in this parable symbolizes the righteousness required to enter the kingdom of heaven, not merely an invitation. In the cultural context, it was customary for a host to provide garments for guests, making the absence of one a sign of rejection or presumption. The phrase “outer darkness” reflects ultimate exclusion from divine presence, characterized by grief and despair. The image of “weeping and gnashing of teeth” further underscores the severity of this separation, capturing profound regret and irreversible loss.

When Jesus quotes, “I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob,” he draws from Exodus 3:6, where God speaks to Moses from the burning bush. The present tense “I am” signifies that these patriarchs, though physically dead, are alive to God, affirming the ongoing relationship between God and his covenant people beyond death. This declaration directly challenges the Sadducees' denial of the resurrection by revealing that God's promises to the patriarchs remain active, requiring their continued existence. It underscores the eternal nature of God's covenant and the certainty of resurrection, aligning divine identity with the living, not the dead.

Jesus' question, “If then David calls him ‘Lord,’ how can he be his son?” references Psalm 110:1, a key messianic passage. In Jewish thought, the Messiah was expected to be a descendant of David, a royal son who would restore Israel. However, by addressing the Messiah as “Lord,” David acknowledges a superior, divine authority, revealing a deeper mystery about the Messiah's identity. This implies that the Christ is not merely a human heir but also a divine figure, greater than David and preexistent in authority—a profound revelation that transcends mere lineage and points to the divine nature of the Messiah.
